up.method: consumed carbon source; residual lactate quantified by HPLC (BioRad HPX-87H) and enzymatic kit over time; numeric time-course only in figures/supplementary, not in text
nitrogen source; residual ammonium measured by kit; found to deplete before lactate (nitrogen limitation); numeric values in figures only

sec.method: excreted then re-consumed (~23-26 h); measured by HPLC; inversely related to O2; numeric values in figures only
transiently accumulated and re-consumed; measured by HPLC; numeric values in figures only

growth_records.cite: Beg et al. Detection of transcriptional triggers in the dynamics of microbial growth: application to the respiratorily versatile bacterium Shewanella oneidensis. Nucleic Acids Research (2012). DOI:10.1093/nar/gks467. [PMC3424579]
growth_records.method: OD curves fit to a logistic growth model (GDM); no numeric growth rate value reported in text
growth_records.snippet: Various growth parameters, viz., temperature (30C), pH (7.2), aeration (1 l/m) and agitation were controlled... dO2 in the vessel was maintained at 20% air saturation level... HPLC was used for quantifying residual lactate and other organic
growth_records.notes: Batch bioreactor growth of S. oneidensis MR-1 on M4 minimal + DL-lactate; M4 composition is in Supplementary Table S1 (not reproduced in text) -> medium_composition_stated:false. Lactate uptake and acetate/pyruvate secretion and ammonium consumption were measured by HPLC/kits, but only qualitative descriptions and time-course figures are given; no numeric mu, doubling time, or specific rate (mmol/gDW/h) appears in the article text, so no rate could be extracted or computed without inventing values. dFBA O2 values (2-8 mM/h) are simulation inputs, not measured fluxes.

growth_records.medium: LMF (lactate mineral medium supplemented with sodium fumarate)

up.method: carbon source in lactate mineral medium; no specific uptake rate reported
alternative electron acceptor; no specific uptake rate reported

growth_records.cite: Stalder et al. Evolving Populations in Biofilms Contain More Persistent Plasmids. Molecular Biology and Evolution (2020). DOI:10.1093/molbev/msaa024. [PMC7253198]
growth_records.snippet: After 4 h the flow of fresh medium was initiated (6 ml/h) and the chemostat culture volume was maintained at 12 ml... The chemostat populations were grown for a total of 485 generations (28 days). All cultures were incubated at 30 C.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat dilution rate D = feed/volume = 6 ml/h / 12 ml = 0.5 /h. At chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.5 /h. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h. Consistency check with reported generations: gen/h = D/ln2 = 0.5/0.6931 = 0.721; over 28 d = 672 h -> 0.721*672 = 485 generations, matching the reported 485 generations. Base lactate mineral medium composition is given only by reference (Paulick et al. 2009), hence medium_composition_stated=false; explicitly stated additions are 0.015 M sodium fumarate and 10 mg/l gentamicin. pH not stated. Oxygen classed microaerophilic because paper states oxygen can be limiting and fumarate is supplied as alternative electron acceptor.

growth_records.strain: S. oneidensis MR-1 (ATCC 700550) + A. humicireducens SgZ-5T (1:1)
growth_records.medium: Anoxic N/Fe-free Burk's medium + 5 mM Fe(III)-citrate, N2 headspace

up.method: co-culture achieved complete reduction of 5 mM Fe(III) within 7 days

up.units: enhanced (acetylene reduction + 15N tracing, Fig 2E/F; numeric value not in main text)
up.method: acetylene reduction + 15N2 tracing; N fixation enhanced in co-culture vs monoculture

sec.method: ~5 mM Fe(II) produced in 7 days (complete reduction)

growth_records.cite: Liu, Xiaohan et al. Synergistic interaction between microbial nitrogen fixation and iron reduction in the environment. The ISME Journal (2025). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wraf212. [PMC12516963]
growth_records.snippet: Compared to monocultures, where S. oneidensis MR-1 and A. humicireducens SgZ-5T achieved only 30% and 14% Fe(III) reduction, respectively, the co-culture exhibited markedly enhanced performance, achieving complete reduction of 5 mM Fe(III)
growth_records.notes: Interspecies syntrophy: diazotroph (SgZ-5T) supplies N enabling DIRB (MR-1) Fe(III) reduction. Co-culture Fe(III) reduction rate = 5 mM / 7 d = 0.714 mM/day (volumetric). Monoculture controls in same medium: MR-1 30% (=1.5 mM, ~0.214 mM/day) and SgZ-5T 14% (=0.7 mM, ~0.10 mM/day) of 5 mM over the incubation. No nitrogenase activity in MR-1 monoculture (Fig S5); co-culture N-fixation enhanced only when Fe(III) present (no enhancement without Fe, Fig S6). No mu/gDW reported.

growth_records.medium: Anoxic N/Fe-free Burk's medium + 5 mM Fe(III)-citrate, N2 headspace (monoculture)

up.method: 30% of 5 mM Fe(III) reduced (=1.5 mM) over ~7 days

growth_records.cite: Liu, Xiaohan et al. Synergistic interaction between microbial nitrogen fixation and iron reduction in the environment. The ISME Journal (2025). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wraf212. [PMC12516963]
growth_records.snippet: where S. oneidensis MR-1 and A. humicireducens SgZ-5T achieved only 30% and 14% Fe(III) reduction, respectively
growth_records.notes: S. oneidensis MR-1 monoculture control. Fe(III) reduction = 0.30 x 5 mM = 1.5 mM over 7 days = 0.214 mM/day (volumetric). No N2 fixation (cannot fix N; no nitrogenase activity Fig S5). N-limited without a diazotroph partner, hence lower Fe reduction than co-culture.

up.method: calculated qS = mu_max,L / Yx/L

sec.method: dFBA-derived: ~33% of lactate uptake flux (acetate concentration measured over time)
dFBA-derived: ~25% of lactate uptake flux (pyruvate concentration measured over time)

mfa.note: dFBA-predicted peak flux at 22-25 h when growth rate is maximal

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: malate synthase (glyoxylate shunt)
mfa.note: EXPERIMENTAL in vitro enzyme assay, mid-log = 0.18+/-0.11 umol/gDCW/min = 0.0108 mmol/gDCW/h

mfa.note: EXPERIMENTAL in vitro enzyme assay, mid-log = 0.90+/-0.18 umol/gDCW/min = 0.054 mmol/gDCW/h

growth_records.cite: Xueyang Feng et al. Integrating Flux Balance Analysis into Kinetic Models to Decipher the Dynamic Metabolism of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. PLoS Computational Biology (2012). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1002376. [PMC3271021]
growth_records.snippet: MR-1 growth displayed an apparent lag phase (~7.1 h) in 30 mM lactate medium (0.1% inoculation)... the lactate-based growth rate (mu_max) was much higher than that for either pyruvate or acetate. mu_max,L = 0.57+/-0.11 h-1; Y_X/L = 17.0+/-1
growth_records.notes: Batch shake-flask culture, 30 C, 150 rpm, ~30 mM lactate, modified MR-1 defined medium (full composition not enumerated in text). mu_max,L=0.57 h-1 (Table 1, fitted from measured biomass/lactate/acetate curves). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.57 = 1.22 h. qLactate = mu/Yx/L = 0.57 h-1 / 0.017 gDCW/mmol = 33.5 mmol/gDCW/h (Yx/L=17.0 gDCW/mol = 0.017 gDCW/mmol). Secretion: text states extracellular acetate ~33% and pyruvate ~25% of the lactate uptake flux before the carbon switch (dFBA split of measured products); acetate 0.33*33.5=11.1, pyruvate 0.25*33.5=8.4 mmol/gDCW/h. Enzyme assays are EXPERIMENTAL in vitro: malate synthase mid-log 0.18+/-0.11 umol/gDCW/min (=0.0108 mmol/gDCW/h), late-log 0.37+/-0.17; malic enzyme mid-log 0.90+/-0.18 (=0.054 mmol/gDCW/h), late-log 1.73+/-0.81. succinyl-CoA synthetase ~6 mmol/gDCW/h is dFBA-predicted (model), not measured. FBA model = iSO783 (cited, Pinchuk et al.). 13C-[3-13C]lactate labeling of proteinogenic amino acids measured by GC-MS TBDMS but used only to validate isotopomer simulations, not to derive net fluxes.

growth_records.medium: Modified MR-1 defined medium

up.method: calculated qS = mu_max,P / Yx/P

growth_records.cite: Xueyang Feng et al. Integrating Flux Balance Analysis into Kinetic Models to Decipher the Dynamic Metabolism of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. PLoS Computational Biology (2012). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1002376. [PMC3271021]
growth_records.snippet: mu_max,P Maximum specific growth rate using pyruvate 0.14+/-0.02 h-1; Y_X/P Apparent biomass yield coefficient from pyruvate 16.7+/-1.3 g DCW/mol pyruvate.
growth_records.notes: Pyruvate secondary-substrate growth phase (Table 1). mu_max,P=0.14 h-1; doubling_time = 0.693/0.14 = 4.95 h. qPyruvate = mu/Yx/P = 0.14 / 0.0167 gDCW/mmol = 8.4 mmol/gDCW/h (Yx/P=16.7 gDCW/mol = 0.0167 gDCW/mmol). Pyruvate is a waste product excreted during lactate growth then re-consumed; parameters fitted from measured extracellular metabolite curves.

growth_records.cite: Xueyang Feng et al. Integrating Flux Balance Analysis into Kinetic Models to Decipher the Dynamic Metabolism of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. PLoS Computational Biology (2012). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1002376. [PMC3271021]
growth_records.snippet: mu_max,A Maximum specific growth rate using acetate 0.13+/-0.02 h-1; Y_X/A Apparent biomass yield coefficient from acetate 11.1+/-4.7 g DCW/mol acetate. The glyoxylate shunt was up-regulated when acetate became the main carbon source.
growth_records.notes: Acetate secondary-substrate growth phase (Table 1). mu_max,A=0.13 h-1; doubling_time = 0.693/0.13 = 5.33 h. qAcetate = mu/Yx/A = 0.13 / 0.0111 gDCW/mmol = 11.7 mmol/gDCW/h (Yx/A=11.1 gDCW/mol = 0.0111 gDCW/mmol). Late-log in vitro enzyme assays are EXPERIMENTAL: malate synthase 0.37+/-0.17 umol/gDCW/min (=0.0222 mmol/gDCW/h), malic enzyme 1.73+/-0.81 umol/gDCW/min (=0.1038 mmol/gDCW/h). Acetate is a waste product excreted during lactate growth then re-consumed.

growth_records.cite: Ford, Kathryne C. et al. Flux-Balance Analysis and CRISPRi-Guided Deletion of a Conditionally Essential Gene (gpmA) in Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. ACS Synthetic Biology (2022). DOI:10.1021/acssynbio.2c00323. [PMC9595118]
growth_records.snippet: We grew the resulting ΔgpmA strain and WT cells in LB in a 96-well plate at 30 °C with shaking and observed that the mutant had a severe growth defect (WT = 0.17 h–1, ΔgpmA = 0.08 h–1).
growth_records.notes: EXPERIMENTAL growth rate for WT MR-1 in LB, aerobic, 30 C, shaking = 0.17 h^-1 (Figure 4 text). Doubling time computed td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.17 = 4.077 h. LB composition not given (mark false). The 'growth potential' table values (0.533-1.105 h^-1) are FBA-PREDICTED maximum specific growth rates in M5 minimal medium, NOT measured, captured in mfa_fluxes with is-model note. Model uptake constraints: lactate -20 mmol/gDW/h, nucleoside -10 mmol/gDW/h (fixed inputs, not measured fluxes). M5 minimal medium recipe (used for FBA/qualitative OD tests, not for the 0.17 h^-1 measurement): 1.29 mM K2HPO4, 1.65 mM KH2PO4, 7.87 mM NaCl, 1.70 mM NH4SO4, 475 uM MgSO4.7H2O, 10 mM HEPES, 1x Wolfe's mineral solution, 1x Wolfe's vitamin solution, pH 7.2; carbon 20 mM D,L-lactate and/or 10 mM nucleoside.

growth_records.cite: Ford, Kathryne C. et al. Flux-Balance Analysis and CRISPRi-Guided Deletion of a Conditionally Essential Gene (gpmA) in Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. ACS Synthetic Biology (2022). DOI:10.1021/acssynbio.2c00323. [PMC9595118]
growth_records.snippet: the mutant had a severe growth defect (WT = 0.17 h–1, ΔgpmA = 0.08 h–1).
growth_records.notes: EXPERIMENTAL growth rate for ΔgpmA in LB, aerobic, 30 C, shaking = 0.08 h^-1 (Figure 4 text), ~47% of WT (severe growth defect). Doubling time td = ln2/0.08 = 0.6931/0.08 = 8.664 h. ΔgpmA cannot grow on lactate alone (FBA growth potential = 0); rescued by simultaneous upper (nucleoside) + lower (lactate) carbon source. Growth-potential values are FBA-PREDICTED (M5 minimal medium), not measured.

growth_records.medium: Modified M1 minimal medium (Kostka and Nealson 1998) with D,L-lactate

up.units: umol O2 CFU^-1 d^-1 (peak during lag phase)
up.method: Clark-type oxygen microsensor in parallel batch bioreactor, cell-specific (normalized by CFU)

up.units: mol O2 total available in 4.2 mL ampule (initial, 218 uM x 4.2 mL)
up.method: calculated from O2 solubility (medium at 89% of water saturation = 218 uM)

sec.method: Inferred: S. oneidensis MR-1 partially oxidizes lactate to acetate + CO2 as dissolved O2 approaches zero (cited Pinchuk et al. 2011); no acetate rate measured in this study
product of full/partial lactate oxidation; inferred not measured

yields.note: Total heat evolved during batch growth = 0.475 J (integrated heat flow). Anabolic heat estimated -2.6e-5 J. Predicted heat if all O2 used for complete lactate oxidation = 0.42 J; if partial oxidation to acetate = 0.45 J.

growth_records.cite: Robador, Alberto et al. Changes in Microbial Energy Metabolism Measured by Nanocalorimetry during Growth Phase Transitions. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00109. [PMC5800293]
growth_records.method: reported (max growth rate 0.24 /h from viable cell counts by drop-plate); doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.24=2.89 h
growth_records.snippet: after the lag phase (~10 h), heat flow increases exponentially up to a maximum of 23.1 uW... Changes in viable cell counts followed the heat flow with a maximum growth rate of 0.24 h-1... Oxygen demand is high during lag phase, peaking at 2
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (gamma-proteobacterium). Batch O2-limited experiment (Figure 1/2). mu=0.24 /h REPORTED (max growth rate from drop-plate viable counts). Doubling time CALCULATED: td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.24=2.89 h. O2 uptake peak 2.8e-3 umol O2 CFU^-1 d^-1 is cell-specific (per CFU), not per gDW; measured with Clark-type microsensor. Cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h without reliable per-cell dry weight per time. Cell-specific max heat output 15.7 uJ CFU^-1 d^-1. Acetate/CO2 secretion inferred from cited Pinchuk et al. 2011, NOT measured here. Medium O2 saturation 89% of water = 218 uM; 4.2 mL ampule holds 9.16e-7 mol O2.

growth_records.medium: Modified M1 minimal medium with 18 mM D,L-lactate (carbon-replete)
growth_records.cite: Robador, Alberto et al. Changes in Microbial Energy Metabolism Measured by Nanocalorimetry during Growth Phase Transitions. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00109. [PMC5800293]
growth_records.method: calculated (steady-state chemostat mu=D=0.05 /h); doubling time td=ln2/0.05=13.86 h
growth_records.snippet: Continuous culture was initiated by pumping medium of the same composition at a dilution rate of 0.05 h-1... pH was maintained at 7.0... dissolved oxygen was maintained at 60% of air saturation
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote. Carbon-replete chemostat baseline. mu=D=0.05 /h at steady state (CALCULATED from reported dilution rate). Doubling time td=ln2/D=0.693/0.05=13.86 h. No per-substrate uptake/secretion rate quantified for chemostat; cells from this chemostat were transferred to nanocalorimeter batch vials for heat measurements.

growth_records.cite: Robador, Alberto et al. Changes in Microbial Energy Metabolism Measured by Nanocalorimetry during Growth Phase Transitions. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00109. [PMC5800293]
growth_records.snippet: Carbon-, nitrogen-, or phosphorus-limiting growth conditions were achieved by decreasing the concentration of D,L-Lactate to 1 mM... Based on the total amount of heat that evolved from the carbon-limited experiment, 0.23 J, less than half a
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote. Carbon (lactate)-limited chemostat, lactate 1 mM. mu=D=0.05 /h (CALCULATED). Downstream nanocalorimeter batch heat 0.23 J (C-limited) vs 0.56 J (C-replete). No numeric uptake/secretion rate; heat used as catabolic proxy. Max cell-specific heat under C/N/P limitation 203-527 uJ cell^-1 d^-1 (>10x replete).

growth_records.notes: Prokaryote. Nitrogen-limited chemostat, NH4Cl 0.1 mM. mu=D=0.05 /h (CALCULATED). Downstream N-limited nanocalorimeter batch: 0.48 J heat, 5.0e5 CFU produced. No numeric uptake/secretion rate quantified.

growth_records.medium: minimal lactate medium (aerobic)

up.method: Combined DL-lactate consumption measured by HPLC over 50 h batch culture; near-simultaneous exhaustion of carbon sources observed. Numeric uptake rate not tabulated in this paper (source data from Beg et al. 2012).
Ammonium depletion measured by HPLC; exhausted nearly simultaneously with carbon sources. No numeric rate in-text.

sec.method: Excretion and subsequent re-uptake of acetate observed in HPLC data (overflow metabolism). Numeric rate not given in this paper.
Excretion and subsequent re-uptake of pyruvate observed in HPLC data. Numeric rate not given in this paper.

growth_records.cite: Collins, Sara B et al. Temporal Expression-based Analysis of Metabolism. PLoS Computational Biology (2012). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1002781. [PMC3510039]
growth_records.method: reported (experimental OD/biomass time-series used to constrain growth flux); no numeric mu stated in this paper
growth_records.snippet: we apply it to data collected during batch growth of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 under minimal lactate aerobic conditions ... The media contained 36 mM L-Lactate, 13 mM D-lactate, 9 mM ammonium, and other minimally required nutrients. The ox
growth_records.notes: Computational (TEAM/dFBA) paper. The prokaryote here is Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 (aerobic, minimal DL-lactate batch). Medium composition explicitly stated (36 mM L-lactate + 13 mM D-lactate + 9 mM ammonium + 10 mM O2 at 100% DO). Experimentally MEASURED behaviors (HPLC): consumption of DL-lactate and ammonium (near-simultaneous exhaustion) and excretion+re-uptake of acetate and pyruvate (overflow metabolism), plus OD-based biomass and microarray expression over 50 h. NO numeric uptake/secretion/growth RATES (mmol/gDW/h or /h) are tabulated in this paper; the underlying quantitative time-series originate from Beg QK, Zampieri M, Klitgord N, Collins SB, Altafini C, et al. (2012) 'Detection of transcriptional triggers in the dynamics of microbial growth: application to the respiratory-versatile bacterium Shewanella oneidensis.' Nucleic Acids Res 40(15):7132-7149. Rates therefore flagged is_experimental:true but rate=null (values live in the cited Beg et al. source, not extractable from this paper text). Temperature and pH not stated.

growth_records.medium: Modified MR-1 medium (Cao et al. 2011) + 20 mM sodium lactate ([3-13C]sodium lactate for MFA)

up.units: substrate for 13C-MFA (relative-flux analysis; absolute rate not reported)
up.method: 13C-MFA on proteinogenic amino-acid isotopomers

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: lactate catabolism flux network
mfa.note: Comparison-group organism; MR-1 flux network was more flexibly changed than PAO1 during biofilm formation (qualitative; no numeric fluxes in text)

yields.note: No numeric yields or absolute fluxes reported for MR-1

growth_records.cite: Wan, Ni et al. Bacterial Metabolism During Biofilm Growth Investigated by 13C Tracing. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.02657. [PMC6255981]
growth_records.snippet: S. oneidensis MR-1 and its high c-di-GMP transconjugant ... were grown in a modified MR-1 medium using 20 mM sodium lactate ... Analysis of amino acid labeling variances ... indicated Shewanella flux network was more flexibly changed than P
growth_records.notes: Comparison-group prokaryote. Grown planktonically (shake flask, 200 rpm) on 20 mM sodium lactate in modified MR-1 medium; [3-13C]lactate used for 13C-fingerprinting MFA. No absolute uptake/growth/secretion rates and no numeric fluxes given in text (only qualitative statement that MR-1 flux network is more flexible than PAO1 between planktonic and biofilm modes). Temperature = room temperature; pH not stated. Recorded for completeness of experimental prokaryote conditions.

growth_records.medium: Defined medium (Elias et al. 2005) with 18 mM lactate, 30 mM PIPES

up.units: mM (consumed; rate not quantified per gDW)
up.method: HPLC/IC organic-acid time course

growth_records.cite: Elias, Dwayne A et al. The influence of cultivation methods on Shewanella oneidensis physiology and proteome expression. Archives of Microbiology (2008). DOI:10.1007/s00203-007-0321-y. [PMC2270922]
growth_records.method: reported (measured from growth curve); 0.15/h at high DO2 initially, rising to ~0.275/h below 20% DO2; td=ln2/0.275=2.52 h
growth_records.snippet: The extended lag and slow growth rates (~0.15/h) in shake flasks correlated with elevated DO2 ... Below 20% DO2 growth rates increased to approximately 0.275/h ... lactate consumption varied significantly as did the transient concentrations
growth_records.notes: S. oneidensis MR-1 shake-flask batch, 18 mM lactate, 30C, 150 rpm, no pH control (30 mM PIPES). Growth biphasic due to O2 mass-transfer limitation: ~0.15/h at high DO2 then ~0.275/h below 20% DO2 (measured). Lactate consumed; pyruvate and acetate transiently produced (HPLC/IC), but no specific rate in mmol/gDW/h reported (concentration profiles only, Fig 1). td=ln2/0.275=2.52 h. Full medium composition cited to Elias et al. 2005, not in this paper.

growth_records.medium: Defined medium (Elias et al. 2005) with 18 mM lactate, 3 mM PIPES

up.units: mM (primary electron donor/C source, consumed to depletion by 18 h)
up.method: HPLC/IC time course

sec.units: mM (transient; secondary electron donor after lactate exhaustion)

growth_records.cite: Elias, Dwayne A et al. The influence of cultivation methods on Shewanella oneidensis physiology and proteome expression. Archives of Microbiology (2008). DOI:10.1007/s00203-007-0321-y. [PMC2270922]
growth_records.method: reported (measured); ~0.275/h, close to O2-limited flask rate; td=ln2/0.275=2.52 h
growth_records.snippet: maintaining a constant DO2 concentration in the controlled batch cultures resulted in a shorter lag phase ... lactate served as the primary electron donor and carbon source, and decreased while pyruvate and acetate accumulated ... When the
growth_records.notes: S. oneidensis MR-1 controlled-batch bioreactor, 18 mM lactate, pH 7.00±0.03, 20% DO2, 30C, 400 rpm (3 mM PIPES). Growth ~0.275/h (measured), ~5 h lag. Lactate fully consumed by 18 h; pyruvate transiently produced then re-consumed as secondary donor; acetate accumulated (~15 mM). No numeric qS/qP in mmol/gDW/h. td=ln2/0.275=2.52 h. Full medium composition cited to Elias et al. 2005.

growth_records.medium: Defined medium (Elias et al. 2005) with 18 mM lactate, 3 mM PIPES
growth_records.cite: Elias, Dwayne A et al. The influence of cultivation methods on Shewanella oneidensis physiology and proteome expression. Archives of Microbiology (2008). DOI:10.1007/s00203-007-0321-y. [PMC2270922]
growth_records.method: reported; at steady state µ = D = 0.1/h; td=ln2/0.1=6.93 h
growth_records.snippet: chemostat cultures operating at steady state were maintained at a constant growth rate (0.1/h) and 20% DO2 throughout the course of the experiment (data not shown) ... fresh medium was pumped into the reactor at a dilution rate of 0.1/h and
growth_records.notes: S. oneidensis MR-1 chemostat at 20% DO2, D=0.1/h so µ=0.1/h at steady state, pH 7, 30C, 3 L, 18 mM lactate feed. Quantitative metabolite/biomass data for this run 'data not shown'; the detailed reproducibility chemostat was run at 50% DO2 (separate record). td=ln2/0.1=6.93 h.

growth_records.medium: Defined medium (Elias et al. 2005) with 18 mM lactate, 3 mM PIPES

up.method: derived from chemostat mass balance qS=D×(Sin−Sout)/X

sec.method: derived qP=D×P/X from steady-state pyruvate 0.14 mM

growth_records.cite: Elias, Dwayne A et al. The influence of cultivation methods on Shewanella oneidensis physiology and proteome expression. Archives of Microbiology (2008). DOI:10.1007/s00203-007-0321-y. [PMC2270922]
growth_records.method: reported/calculated; steady-state µ=D=0.1/h ('as above' = same parameters as 0.1/h chemostat); td=ln2/0.1=6.93 h
growth_records.snippet: Lactate, acetate, fumarate and succinate were all below detectable limits suggesting ... carbon-limited during steady state growth. Pyruvate was present in both chemostatic cultures at 0.14 ± 0.02 mM. Dry weight biomass measurements confirm
growth_records.notes: S. oneidensis MR-1 duplicate chemostats at 50% DO2, steady state (100 h), pH 7, 30C, 3 L, 18 mM lactate feed. D=0.1/h assumed from 'as above'/identical-parameters statement (explicit D given for the companion chemostat), so µ=0.1/h. Measured steady-state values: biomass X=338.0±4.3 mg/L=0.338 gDW/L; residual lactate below detection (carbon-limited) so Sout≈0, Sin=18 mM; pyruvate 0.14±0.02 mM (acetate/fumarate/succinate below detection). DERIVED qLactate = D×(Sin−Sout)/X = 0.1 h-1 × 18 mmol/L / 0.338 gDW/L = 5.33 mmol/gDW/h. DERIVED qPyruvate (secretion) = D×P/X = 0.1 × 0.14 / 0.338 = 0.041 mmol/gDW/h. Yxs = X/(lactate consumed) = 0.338 gDW/L / 18 mmol/L = 0.0188 gDW/mmol = 18.8 gDW/mol; per gram (lactate 90 g/mol → 1.62 g/L) = 0.338/1.62 = 0.209 gDW/g. td=ln2/0.1=6.93 h. qS/qP flagged medium confidence because D for the 50% DO2 run is inferred (not restated numerically).
